name: Sol
notes: Our star. Wikipedia article is 'Sun'.

name: Proxima Centauri
notes: Closest star to Sol (1.30 ly). Red dwarf flare star. Hosts Proxima Centauri b, one of the nearest known exoplanets in a habitable zone.

name: Toliman
notes: Secondary component of Alpha Centauri (K1V). The name 'Toliman' is the IAU-approved name for Alpha Centauri B. Wikipedia covers the whole system in one article.

name: Rigil Kentaurus
notes: Primary component of Alpha Centauri, the nearest star system. Wikipedia has one article for the whole system. Sun-like G2V star.

name: Barnard's Star
notes: Second closest star system to Sol (1.82 ly). Fastest known proper motion of any star. Red dwarf. Candidate exoplanet Barnard's Star b announced 2018.

name: Wolf 359
notes: One of the nearest stars (2.39 ly). Red dwarf flare star. Famous from Star Trek: The Next Generation.

name: Lalande 21185
notes: Red dwarf, one of the brightest red dwarfs in the night sky. At 2.55 ly, one of the nearest stars.

name: Sirius
notes: Brightest star in the night sky (apparent magnitude -1.46). A1V main sequence star. Wikipedia covers the whole system.

name: Luyten 726-8 A
notes: Component A of binary system Luyten 726-8 (Gliese 65). Variable star designation BL Ceti. Wikipedia article is 'Luyten 726-8' (redirected from 'Gliese 65').

name: Ross 154
notes: Red dwarf flare star in Sagittarius.

name: Ross 248
notes: Red dwarf in Andromeda. In about 36,000 years will briefly become the closest star to the Sun.

name: Epsilon Eridani
notes: One of the nearest Sun-like stars. 'Ran' is the IAU proper name, but the star is far more commonly known as Epsilon Eridani. Has a confirmed Jupiter-like planet and a debris disk.

name: Lacaille 9352
notes: Red dwarf in Piscis Austrinus. One of the closest stars with a high proper motion. Hosts two candidate super-Earth exoplanets.

name: Ross 128
notes: Red dwarf in Virgo. Hosts Ross 128 b, one of the nearest temperate Earth-sized exoplanets.

name: EZ Aquarii A
notes: Component A of the triple star system EZ Aquarii (Gliese 866 / Luyten 789-6). Wikipedia article is 'EZ Aquarii'.

name: 61 Cygni B
notes: Component B of 61 Cygni.

name: 61 Cygni A
notes: Component A of 61 Cygni, a famous binary star. First star to have its distance measured by parallax (Friedrich Bessel, 1838). Wikipedia article is '61 Cygni'.

name: Procyon
notes: Brightest star in Canis Minor and 8th brightest in the night sky. F5IV-V subgiant/main-sequence star.

name: Struve 2398 A
notes: Component A of binary system Struve 2398 (Gliese 725) in Draco. Both components host exoplanets.

name: Struve 2398 B
notes: Component B of binary system Struve 2398 (Gliese 725) in Draco.

name: Groombridge 34
notes: Component A of Groombridge 34, a binary red dwarf system in Andromeda. Has a confirmed super-Earth exoplanet.

name: DX Cancri
notes: Red dwarf flare star in Cancer. One of the faintest known nearby stars.

name: Epsilon Indi
notes: Orange dwarf (K5V) in Indus. Has a confirmed Jupiter-mass planet and a pair of brown dwarf companions (Ba and Bb). High proper motion star.

name: Tau Ceti
notes: Sun-like star (G8.5V) in Cetus. One of the nearest single G-type stars. Has a debris disk and candidate planets. Popular target in science fiction.

name: GJ 1061
notes: Red dwarf in Horologium. Hosts three Earth-mass planets; GJ 1061 d is in the habitable zone. Old star (7+ billion years).

name: YZ Ceti
notes: Red dwarf flare star in Cetus. Hosts three confirmed planets. Possible first detection of a magnetic field from a terrestrial exoplanet (2023).

name: Luyten's Star
notes: Red dwarf in Canis Minor. Named after Willem Jacob Luyten. Hosts Luyten b, a potentially habitable super-Earth.

name: Kapteyn's Star
notes: Red subdwarf with the highest known proper motion of any star prior to Barnard's Star. Believed to originate from the Omega Centauri globular cluster.

name: Lacaille 8760
notes: Red dwarf in Microscopium. One of the brightest red dwarfs visible in the night sky.

name: Kruger 60
notes: Component A of Kruger 60, a binary red dwarf system in Cepheus. Wikipedia covers the system under 'Kruger 60'.

name: Ross 614
notes: Component A of the Ross 614 binary system. Low-mass red dwarf in Monoceros.

name: Wolf 1061
notes: Red dwarf in Ophiuchus. Hosts three confirmed planets; Wolf 1061c was one of the nearest known potentially habitable exoplanets at time of discovery.

name: Van Maanen's Star
notes: Closest known solitary white dwarf to the Sun. Third white dwarf discovered (1917). In Pisces.

name: Wolf 424 A
notes: Component A of Wolf 424, a binary red dwarf system in Virgo. Both components are near the hydrogen-burning limit.

name: Gl 1
notes: Red dwarf in Sculptor. The first entry in the Gliese Catalogue of Nearby Stars.

name: TZ Arietis
notes: Red dwarf flare star in Aries. Very high flare activity. Three candidate planets reported (2019).

name: Gl 687
notes: Red dwarf in Draco. Hosts a Neptune-mass planet (Gliese 687 b) discovered in 2014.

name: Gl 674
notes: Red dwarf in Ara with an exoplanetary companion. Flare star.

name: LHS 292
notes: Red dwarf in Sextans. Also designated GJ 3622. One of the faintest nearby stars.

name: Gl 440
notes: White dwarf (DQ type) in Musca. Also known as LAWD 37 / LP 145-141. Fourth closest white dwarf to the Sun. Remnant of a massive B-type star.

name: Gl 876
notes: Red dwarf in Aquarius with four confirmed exoplanets. First red dwarf confirmed to have a planet (Gliese 876 b, 1998). System exhibits rare Laplace resonance.

name: GJ 1245A
notes: Component A of triple red dwarf system in Cygnus. Both A and B are flare stars (collectively V1581 Cygni).

name: DENIS J1048-3956
notes: Ultra-cool red dwarf in Antlia. One of the smallest and dimmest known stars near the Sun. Also designated GJ 3618.

name: GJ 1002
notes: Red dwarf in Cetus. Hosts two Earth-mass planets in the habitable zone, discovered 2022.

name: Groombridge 1618
notes: Red dwarf in Ursa Major. One of the nearest K-type stars. Possible unconfirmed planetary companion.

name: Lalande 21258 A
notes: Component A of the Gliese 412 binary system in Ursa Major. Also known as Lalande 21258.

name: Gl 832
notes: Red dwarf in Grus. Hosts one confirmed Jupiter-mass planet (Gliese 832 b).

name: GJ 1005A
notes: Component A of a red dwarf binary system in Cetus.

name: Gl 682
notes: Red dwarf in Scorpius. Two candidate habitable-zone planets were reported in 2014 but not confirmed.

name: 40 Eridani A
notes: Primary of the 40 Eridani triple system. 'Keid' is the IAU-approved name for component A, but '40 Eridani' is more commonly used. Wikipedia article is '40 Eridani'. The system includes a white dwarf (B) and a red dwarf (C).

name: EV Lacertae
notes: Red dwarf flare star in Lacerta. Young star (~300 Myr) with a magnetic field much stronger than the Sun's. Capable of extremely powerful flares.

name: 70 Ophiuchi A
notes: Primary of the 70 Ophiuchi binary system. Orange dwarf (K0V). One of the first confirmed gravitationally-bound binary stars (William Herschel). Wikipedia article is '70 Ophiuchi'.

name: Altair
notes: 12th brightest star in the night sky. A7V main sequence star. Rapid rotator (flattened shape confirmed by interferometry). Part of the Summer Triangle asterism.

name: EI Cancri A
notes: Component A of the EI Cancri (GJ 1116 / G 9-38) binary system in Cancer. Near the bottom of the main sequence. Extremely high flare rate (~5 per hour). Wikipedia article title is 'EI Cancri'.

name: Gl 445
notes: Red dwarf in Camelopardalis. Voyager 1 will pass within 1.6 ly of this star in ~40,000 years. Rapidly approaching the Sun.

name: GJ 3323
notes: Red dwarf in Eridanus with 2 detected planets (2017). Passed within 7.3 ly of Sun ~104,000 years ago.

name: Gl 526
notes: Red dwarf in Bootes. Also known as Wolf 498 / Lalande 25372.

name: Stein 2051 A
notes: Red dwarf component A of Stein 2051, a binary system in Camelopardalis. Wikipedia article is 'Stein 2051'. Nearest known separate red dwarf + white dwarf binary.

name: Gliese 251
notes: Red dwarf in Gemini. Also known as Wolf 294 / HD 265866. Nearest star in Gemini. Hosts a confirmed super-Earth (Gliese 251 b) and a second planet in the habitable zone confirmed in 2025.

name: LP 816-60
notes: Red dwarf (M4) in Capricornus.

name: Gl 205
notes: Red dwarf in Orion. No confirmed planets.

name: Gl 229
notes: Red dwarf in Lepus. Host of Gliese 229B, the first confirmed brown dwarf orbiting a star (discovered 1995). Gliese 229B was later found to itself be a binary brown dwarf (resolved 2024).

name: Sigma Draconis
notes: 'Alsafi' is the IAU-approved name, but the star is more commonly known as Sigma Draconis. Sun-like K0V star. Wikipedia article title is 'Sigma Draconis'.

name: Ross 47
notes: Red dwarf in Orion. BY Draconis variable (V1352 Orionis). Nearest star in Orion.

name: Gl 570A
notes: Primary of the Gliese 570 quaternary system (also known as 33 G. Librae). Orange dwarf with two red dwarf companions (B and C) and a T-dwarf brown dwarf (D). The brown dwarf was one of the coolest known at discovery.

name: Gl 693
notes: Red dwarf.

name: Gl 754
notes: Red dwarf in Telescopium.

name: Gl 908
notes: BY Draconis variable (BR Piscium) in Pisces. Metal-deficient red dwarf.

name: Wolf 1055
notes: Component A of the Gliese 752 binary system in Aquila. Also known as Wolf 1055.

name: Gl 588
notes: Red dwarf in Lupus with very stable light flux.

name: Achird
notes: Wikipedia uses Eta Cassiopeiae. G0V star similar to Sun. Named Achird (matching data). 472-year binary orbit. Both components in one article.

name: Gl 570B
notes: Red dwarf, part of Gliese 570 quaternary system. Forms close binary with component C. Covered in system article.

name: Guniibuu
notes: Wikipedia uses '36 Ophiuchi'. Triple system of K dwarfs. Component A named Guniibuu (Kamilaroi/Euahlayi Aboriginal name for robin red-breast). All three components in one article.

name: Gl 664
notes: Component C of 36 Ophiuchi (also known as 30 Scorpii). Covered in the 36 Ophiuchi system article.

name: HD 155876
notes: Wikipedia title is HD 155876 (redirected from Furuhjelm 46). Binary of two similar red dwarfs in Hercules. Nearest true star system in Hercules. Both components in one article.

name: YZ Canis Minoris
notes: Wikipedia uses YZ Canis Minoris. Powerful flare star, roughly three times the size of Jupiter.

name: HR 7703
notes: Wikipedia uses HR 7703. Binary in Sagittarius. Approaching Sun at 129 km/s, closest approach in 41,100 years at 6.7 ly. Both components in one article.

name: 82 G. Eri
notes: Wikipedia uses '82 G. Eridani' (matching display name). G6V star with 3 confirmed super-Earths. High-velocity Population II star, possibly very old. Dust disk detected.

name: Gl 268
notes: Binary red dwarf system in Auriga. Variable star designation QY Aurigae. Flare star discovered 1975.

name: Delta Pavonis
notes: Wikipedia uses Delta Pavonis. G8 IV subgiant (evolving off main sequence). Identified as 'Best SETI target' by Turnbull & Tarter. Metal-rich.

name: Gl 784
notes: Red dwarf in Telescopium. Young (~0.85 Gyr). Will approach to 11.4 ly in ~121,700 years.

name: Gl 644A
notes: Primary component of V1054 Ophiuchi (Wolf 630) quintuple system. Covered in system article.

name: G 240-72
notes: Wikipedia uses G 240-72. White dwarf (DQP9.0) in Draco. 7th closest white dwarf. Massive at 0.81 solar masses. Cooling age 5.69 Gyr.

name: HN Librae
notes: Wikipedia uses HN Librae as the primary name. Red dwarf in Libra with a super-Earth in habitable zone.

name: EQ Pegasi
notes: Wikipedia uses EQ Pegasi. Binary of two red dwarf flare stars. Primary has one known exoplanet. Subject of 1998 alien signal hoax. Both components in one article.

name: Gl 581
notes: Famous exoplanet host in Libra. 3 confirmed planets (b, c, e), 2 disputed (d, g). One of oldest, least active M dwarfs known. Subject of intense habitability debate.

name: ADS 7251 A
notes: Component A of the Gliese 338 / ADS 7251 binary system. Two near-identical red dwarfs. Component B hosts a planet. Wikipedia article title is 'ADS 7251'.

name: Gl 338B
notes: Component B of ADS 7251. Hosts an exoplanet discovered 2020. Covered in system article.

name: LP 658-2
notes: Wikipedia title is LP 658-2 but URL is Gliese_223.2. White dwarf in Orion. 8th closest white dwarf.

name: Gl 625
notes: Red dwarf in Draco with a super-Earth (GJ 625 b) near the inner edge of the habitable zone, detected 2017 by HARPS-N.

name: Gl 643
notes: Part of the V1054 Ophiuchi/Wolf 630 quintuple system. Separated ~465 AU from the V1054 Ophiuchi AB pair. Covered in the V1054 Ophiuchi article.

name: GJ 1128
notes: Red dwarf in Carina with a prominent 5-year starspot cycle comparable to the Sun's. Distance recognized in 2005.

name: HD 219134
notes: Wikipedia uses HD 219134. K3V star in Cassiopeia with 5+ known planets, including 2 transiting rocky super-Earths. Naked-eye star (mag 5.57). Very old (~11 Gyr).

name: Gl 408
notes: Red dwarf in Leo.

name: Xi Bootis
notes: Wikipedia uses Xi Bootis (Xi Boötis). Nearest visible star in Bootes. BY Draconis variable. Young system (~200 Myr). Both components in one article.

name: Gl 299
notes: Red dwarf in Cancer.

name: Gl 829
notes: Double-lined spectroscopic binary of two red dwarfs in Pegasus. Also known as Ross 775. High proper motion.

name: GJ 3522
notes: Nearby triple star system (two spectroscopic binary components plus an outer companion). Also known as G 41-14.

name: Gl 667A
notes: Triple star system in Scorpius. K3V primary. All three components covered in one article. Component C has confirmed super-Earths including Cc in habitable zone.

name: Gl 880
notes: Red dwarf in Pegasus. Earlier planet candidate likely spurious (stellar activity artifact).

name: EE Leonis
notes: Wikipedia title redirects to EE Leonis. BY Draconis variable in Leo. Also known as Wolf 358.

name: Gl 393
notes: Red dwarf in Sextans with an exoplanet. Chromospherically inactive, possibly ~10 Gyr old.

name: Gl 809
notes: Red dwarf in Cepheus.

name: 268 G. Cet
notes: Triple system in Cetus. Component A is K-type (HD 16160). Display name '268 G. Cet' is less common than Gliese 105. All three components in one article.

name: HD 4628
notes: Wikipedia titles the article HD 4628. K2.5V main-sequence star in Pisces, mag 5.7, just visible to naked eye. Display name '96 G. Psc' is less standard than HD 4628.

name: HU Delphini
notes: Binary red dwarf in Delphinus with 538.6-day period. Primary is fully convective at 23.7% solar mass.

name: Beta Hydri
notes: Brightest star in Hydrus at mag 2.8. G2 IV subgiant, a preview of the Sun's future. One of the oldest nearby stars (6-7.5 Gyr).

name: GJ 3991
notes: Binary in Hercules: red dwarf + white dwarf in tight 14.71-day orbit. Also known as G 203-47.

name: TW Piscis Austrini
notes: Also known as Fomalhaut B. K-type star gravitationally bound to Fomalhaut despite 0.91 ly separation.

name: Gl 514
notes: M-type star in Virgo. Has super-Earth Gliese 514 b on eccentric 140-day orbit partially in habitable zone (discovered 2022).

name: 107 Piscium
notes: K1V star in Pisces, ~6 Gyr old. Mag 5.14-5.26, visible to naked eye.

name: Mu Cassiopeiae A
notes: Binary system. High-velocity star moving at 167 km/s through the galaxy. G5V subdwarf primary. One of first high-velocity stars identified. System article covers both A and B.

name: Vega
notes: 5th brightest star in the night sky (mag 0.03). Key calibration star. Former and future pole star (~13,700 CE). Has circumstellar debris disk. Rapid rotator at 236 km/s.

name: Fomalhaut
notes: One of the brightest stars in the sky. Famous debris disk. Fomalhaut b was thought to be an exoplanet but is now understood as collision debris. Triple system with TW PsA and LP 876-10.

name: Gl 673
notes: K7V orange dwarf in Ophiuchus. In the 'sweet spot' between solar analogs and M dwarfs for habitability studies.

name: Pi3 Orionis
notes: Wikipedia article title is Pi3 Orionis. IAU-approved name is Tabit (current display name). F6V at mag 3.16 in Orion's club. Spectral anchor point.

name: GJ 1151
notes: Red dwarf in Ursa Major. Notable for 2020 detection of radio emissions consistent with star-planet magnetic interaction. Confirmed planet GJ 1151 c on 390-day orbit.

name: Gl 623
notes: Binary red dwarf in Hercules, 1.9 AU separation. Imaged by Hubble's Faint Object Camera in 1994.

name: Chi Draconis
notes: Mag 3.6 binary. F7V primary + ~K0V secondary. System article covers both components.

name: Gar
notes: Red dwarf in Virgo, formally named Gar (display name matches). Hosts rocky super-Earth Gliese 486 b studied by JWST; dayside ~865 K, likely no atmosphere.

name: Gl 300
notes: No dedicated Wikipedia article found. Faint red dwarf.

name: Gl 686
notes: Red dwarf M1V in Hercules with one known super-Earth on 15.5-day orbit.

name: Gl 293
notes: White dwarf in Volans. Nearest star in that constellation. 12th nearest white dwarf. Cooling age ~2.65 Gyr.

name: p Eridani B
notes: Component B of p Eridani binary. K2V, 0.76 solar masses. 394-year orbital period. Single article covers both components.

name: Gl 54
notes: Binary red dwarf system in Tucana with ~1 year orbital period. Nearest neighbors are Zeta Tucanae (3.1 ly) and Beta Hydri (5.1 ly).

name: p Eridani A
notes: Component A of p Eridani binary. K2V, 0.77 solar masses. Two nearly identical stars.

name: Van Maanen 2
notes: Closest known solitary white dwarf. Third white dwarf ever identified (after 40 Eri B and Sirius B). DZ8 spectral class, prototype for DZ white dwarfs.

name: Mu Herculis A
notes: Quadruple star system. G5 IV subgiant primary expanding toward giant phase. Parameters determined via asteroseismology. System article covers all components.

name: Beta Canum Venaticorum
notes: Wikipedia article title is Beta Canum Venaticorum. IAU proper name Chara (current display name). G0V solar analog, spectral anchor point. Name means 'joy' (Greek) or 'beloved' (Latin).

name: 61 Virginis
notes: G7V solar twin in Virgo with three confirmed super-Earth/Neptune exoplanets, all within Venus-distance. Near-solar composition.

name: Gl 877
notes: Red dwarf M3V in Octans; nearest star in that constellation. ~14x more luminous than Proxima Centauri.

name: Zeta Tucanae
notes: F9.5V solar-type star at mag 4.20. Has debris disk detected in infrared. ~5.3 Gyr old.

name: Chi1 Orionis
notes: G0V star at the tip of Orion's club. Candidate Ursa Major Moving Group member. RS CVn variable.

name: Gl 250A
notes: K3.5V star in Monoceros. Binary with M2.5V companion. System article covers both A and B.

name: 41 G. Arae A
notes: Primary of hierarchical triple in Ara. G8V, also HD 156274. System article covers all components.

name: HD 192310
notes: Wikipedia article titled HD 192310 (5 G. Capricorni). K-type star in Capricornus with two Neptune-sized exoplanets.

name: Gl 849
notes: M3.5V red dwarf in Aquarius with two gas giant companions. First long-period Jupiter-like planet around a red dwarf discovered here (2006).

name: Ross 695
notes: Wikipedia article titled Ross 695. M2V red dwarf; nearest star in Corvus. No planets detected.

name: Gl 867A
notes: Quadruple system in Aquarius. Gl 867A (FK Aqr) is a spectroscopic binary with strongest known dipolar magnetic field for its mass class (as of 2024).

name: Gamma Leporis A
notes: F6V star in Lepus at mag 3.59. Ursa Major Moving Group member. High-priority Terrestrial Planet Finder target. Binary with AK Leporis.

name: Gl 433
notes: Red dwarf M2V in Hydra with three exoplanets. Gliese 433 d in optimistic habitable zone. Gliese 433 c is nearest/widest/coldest Neptune-like detected (as of 2020).

name: Delta Eridani
notes: Wikipedia article titled Delta Eridani. IAU name Rana (current display name). Mag 3.54, 5th brightest in Eridanus. Name means 'frog' from a historical misattribution.

name: Groombridge 1830
notes: Famous high proper motion star ('Flying Star'). Once held the record before Kapteyn's Star and Barnard's Star. G/K subdwarf.

name: Beta Comae Berenices
notes: G0V solar-type star at mag 4.3 in Coma Berenices. Spectral anchor point. Actually brighter than Alpha Comae Berenices despite Bayer designation.

name: Gamma Pavonis
notes: F9V solar-type star in Pavo at mag 4.22. Ranked 14th on TPF-C's top 100 targets for rocky habitable-zone planets.

name: Kappa1 Ceti
notes: Young (300-400 Myr) G5V star. BY Dra variable with 9-day rotation. Excellent match for the young Sun at a key epoch in Earth's past.

name: HD 102365
notes: Wikipedia article titled HD 102365 (66 G. Centauri). Ancient G-type star (11-13 Gyr). Previously reported planet likely a false positive (2025 study).

name: Gl 357
notes: M-type star in Hydra with three exoplanets. Gliese 357 d is a super-Earth in the habitable zone. JWST found no clear atmospheric molecules on planet b.

name: Gl 176
notes: Red dwarf in Taurus (nearest star in constellation). Hosts super-Earth Gliese 176 b orbiting inside the star's inner magnetosphere.

name: HD 100623
notes: Wikipedia article titled HR 4458 (HD 100623, 20 Crateris). K0V star in Hydra, ~5 Gyr old. Binary system.

name: 61 Ursae Majoris
notes: G8V solar-type star in Ursa Major at mag 5.35. ~2 Gyr old, 93% solar mass.

name: Gl 569A
notes: Flare star in Bootes orbited by a pair of brown dwarfs (Gliese 569 Ba/Bb in 2.4-year orbit). Complex hierarchical system.
